sentences.text: Getting to Paris-- I didn't have much money in my pocket.
I went to see the Eiffel Tower by, by Metro, saw loads and loads of German soldiers everywhere, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and went to see-- I went to see Champs-Elysees.
All by bus, I went around.
My French was still not good.
It was not-- I could converse, but I had a terrible accent.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Naturally, I was young.
But what does one thin when one is young-- women, nightlife.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to Place Pigalle, which was the center, all the cabarets and all the nightlife and nude shows and, and anything, you know, in this, in this field.
I came up the Metro on Place Pigalle.
And I noticed, again, full of Germans in, in uniform.
And I noticed there a tall fellow, Mediterranean-looking, talking to-- is around him, German soldiers.
So I went there to listen to him nearby.
And, and, I heard him say, you know, you have to see the, the, the cabaret, [?
Eve ?] Cabaret with beautiful naked women dancing, and this here.
And he took a group and went down Pigalle, Rue Pigalle.
And I was intrigued.
And I followed him.
And he took them to a cabaret, got them inside.
And I saw him to give the-- there was always a porter outside, you know, gave him a piece of paper.
And the, the soldiers went inside.
And then he went back to Place Pigalle, speaking again to soldiers.
I said, that's interesting.
That guy looks a little bit Jewish.
Maybe, maybe he's a Jew.
And I said-- I went up to him and said, excuse me.
You know, I saw you talking to the Germans.
Your German is pretty good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you're taking them to a nightclub?
He said, yes.
I'm a guide for the night, nightlife of Paris.
And I'm taking German soldiers into the nightclubs.
And whatever they consume, I'm coming later on in the night.
And I'm getting commission, whatever they are consuming.
And the, I said, this is fantastic.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Said, look, you know, I'm from Alsace-Lorraine.
And I've, I haven't got much money.
And how, how about if I-- I speak German.
How about if I join you here?
He said, I must tell you.
There are so many soldiers, I really don't mind.
But if you are more or less in competition with me, you, you can do it.
But you have to give me a percentage of what you are earning.
I said, OK.
That's fine.
And he introduced me to all the nightclubs.
He introduced me to the private shows, sex shows.
He introduced me to brothels.
And I started as the guide of the nightlife of Paris in Place Pigalle.
We became actually very friendly.
And I shared an apartment with him.
He actually was from-- he was from Greece.
His name was Henri.
Again, I forgot his surname, but Henri.
He came-- he was a Greek fellow.
And I never let on that I was a Jew.
But I'm a young fellow from Alsace-Lorraine.
And I must say that this particular job that I did brought me quite a bit of money.
And I met a lot of people, some real French people.
One was a fellow form Corsica, Raymond, who was a pimp.
He had a few girls on his strings.
And he and I was extremely friendly.
And one day, I told him that I was a Jew.
He said, you must be mad.
You are taking Germans to the nightlife?
And what identification you have?
I said, I have my German passport.
He said, you must be mad.
You're crazy.
He arranged for me to have a false identification paper from Alsace-Lorraine.
I was called Robert Metzner, from Metz.
It was with a photo on it and everything, a proper identification paper, which I presume was-- looked all right.
And he somehow did this for me without asking me for any money.
I had some very-- I met some-- a lot of patriotic people who were absolutely anti-German.
But in that field, they had to work in the nightclubs.
And, and, and somehow or other, I became now Robert, and known to everybody.
And then I-- once I had the identification paper, my friend from Greece had to know.
Because he called me Alfred, you know?
I said, look here.
From now on-- I told him the truth.
And he somehow, because we were very friendly.
And he-- it didn't affect him.
As far as he was concerned, he was doing a job earning money out of the, out of the Germans.
[FRENCH].
You thought he was, you thought he was Jewish to begin with, but he wasn't.
Yeah, but he wasn't.
No.
You know, he always says, [FRENCH] this here and [FRENCH] this.
So he was anti-German.
But he earned money from out of them.
So did I.
[SIGHS] It was a quite-- something exciting happened.
Once, I was standing at Place Pigalle.
And I tackled a German civilian.
In-- they had very long leather coats on.
And I wanted to show him nightlife in Paris.
He said, oh, you speak German very well.
I said, well, I'm from Alsace-Lorraine.
He said, oh, OK.
Come with me.
And he introduced-- he let me show, show them cabarets.
And he said, look, you must join me.
Have a drink with me.
And we drank together in the cabaret.
And he ordered champagne, seemed to have a lot of money.
And then he said to me, look here, I am really not interested in girls.
But you seem to be a nice fellow.
I said, wait a minute.
I'm a guide for the nightlife of Paris.
I show you whatever you want.
But I'm not interested in men.
He said, it's a pity.
Said, but I have some contacts where I can introduce you.
And I went, inquired this.
We met again the next day, and found out through that friend, Raymond, the, the, the Corsican friend, what there is, that there is something for homosexuals.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So the next day, or a few days later, he came again to Place Pigalle.
And we met.
And he-- I took him there.
And I left him there.
And I made arrangements before that, that I would get commission whatever he consumes, et cetera, because he had to pay a lot of money for this.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I left him there.
And I went out.
And he wanted me to stay with him.
I said, no, I'm not really interested in that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Another experience I had was when I was stopped by another civilian on Place Pigalle.
And he asked me for identification papers.
And I showed him.
Ah, you're from Alsace-Lorraine, so you speak German.
I said, yes.
What you doing here?
I'm showing the-- I'm the guide for Paris nightlife.
I said, I'm taking German soldiers into cabarets, et cetera.
He said, come with me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We went to Place de l'Opera, which was the Gestapo headquarters.
He took me in there, took me up to a higher officer, and he looked at me, my identification paper.
And he said, you know, we don't like this at all, that you show the nightlife to our soldiers.
They can find it themselves.
They go to Pigalle, go to cabarets, but I don't like this relationship between guides.
And you're not an official guide.
You're-- somehow, we don't want you to continue that.
And if we see you again there, we will arrest you and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then he said-- and this is a funny part of it.
He showed me a Hebrew letter, which he had on his desk.
He said, you know what this is?
I said, yeah.
It's, it's Arabic, looks Arabic.
He said, no no, that's Hebrew.
I am an expert on Jewish, on, on, on Jews and the physiognomy of Jews.
And I can see from their head, the nose and the physiognomy, whether someone is Jewish or not.
And he said-- and he touched my head.
And he says, I can see, you are not a Jew.
You are from Alsace-Lorraine.
You a real Aryan.
I said, yes, naturally.
He said, look here.
You go.
But don't you ever again go back to Place Pigalle and take soldiers to, [SIGHS] to the nightlife.
I went and saw my friend, Raymond, the Corsican friend.
And I told him this story.
He said, look, now it's getting hot.
You mustn't do that anymore.
You mustn't do that.
I tell you, I will introduce you to somebody from the resistance.
And he went with me.
I met [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a man.
He said, look here.
I want you to go to a place, which is in the occupied zone near Macon.
[SIGHS] I went to see that fellow.
And I told him again the truth.
And he introduced me up the hill.

It was down the Montagnes Saint-Cyr, near Macon, which-- and I found a lot of young people there, people who didn't want to work in Germany because-- I don't know whether you realize, the young people had to go-- if the Germans wanted to-- had to go and work in Germany as, as workers, laborers.
And a lot of young people didn't want to work in Germany, a lot of communists and socialists, and quite a few Jews there.
We were hiding up in the, [INAUDIBLE], in the mountain, in caves.
We were organizing food from peasants who had-- were surrounding us.
Sometimes we had to steal food.
Sometimes we had to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever money we had, you know, go and get-- buy some food in, in villages.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this-- in 1942, I believe, I joined.
In 1942, I joined the resistance.
Just a second.
Actually, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was in-- somewhere in summer, in summer, 19-- beginning, 1943, actually.
And we didn't do very much.
Once, we blew up a, a, a, a train.
Or we tried to blew up a train with, with German soldiers.
I do not know really what the result was.
Because as soon as we placed the explosions on the, on the rail and the train was coming, we all run away.
But we understand later on that the train derailed.
But apart from injuries, we didn't, unfortunately, kill any soldiers.
This was the only thing that really I remember vividly.
The most of the time, we were hiding.
Really, the resistance movement really only started when the invasion-- when, when the Allied troops invaded France.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As I was speaking German, I was-- always had the job to go from a resistance group to another one by train and deliver messages by word of mouth.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in that village where we were, I was-- befriended a young lady.
And we were quite close together.
In one of the weak moments of love, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told her that I was Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Later on, a few weeks later, I had enough of her.
We broke off.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went again on a trip, a train.
And on one trip, the train stopped in the middle of the field, surrounded by French troops, French police and, and army, surrounded-- put tables up in the field.
And we were-- we all had to show our identification papers.
And when it came for me to show, looked at the identification paper.
And he laughed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He said, it's not real.
It's not real.
It's a false identification paper.
Who are you?
I said, well, I'm from Alsace-Lorraine.
It says here, you know.
No, you're not.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You sound really like a German.
Maybe you are a German deserter.
I said, no, not at all.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
They took me by car to the Gestapo.
And the Gestapo, started interviewing me.
I still told them I'm from Alsace-Lorraine.
And they starting torturing me.
Well, this is what I thought.
Because to jump the gun a little bit, when I went back after the war to find that girl, apparently soon after I left, she disappeared.
She was gone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, when they started-- the Germans started torturing me and hitting me in the face, I said, look.
They will want to know about my resistance group.
And they would want to know names.
And I'm not prepared to give names under, under torture.
So I said to the Gestapo, I said, look.
I will tell you the truth.
I'm from Vienna.
I'm a Jew.
I was hiding up in the hills, up in the hill.
And, and really, I have nothing to do with, with the resistance.
And somebody gave me a false identification paper.
I paid for it.
And this is-- they put me in jail, checked with the-- with Vienna, with Austria, to see whether this is correct.
And naturally, it was.
And they took me by, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by a prisoners' van to near Paris, to Drancy.
Drancy was approximately [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] just outside Paris, in the suburbs of Paris.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We arrived in Drancy.
It was a camp.
It was actually like a block of flats, quite many rooms.
We had mattresses on the floor.
And there were many-- 50, 60 people in each hall.
It was organized.
It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] organized by Jews.
It was administered by Jews.
And the French were guarding the camp.
Nobody was allowed to leave.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got food.
And we were told that from time to time, they are taking people to work in Germany, away, away from France to work.
And this is what will happen to us.
Sure.
Well, you see, I had to judge myself, whether it is better to be tortured and maybe under torture, to tell them names of the resistance group that I was, was.
Or whether it was, was better to tell the truth.
I didn't want to get tortured.
I didn't want to get-- didn't know whether I could stand pain.
So I-- so they, they said-- I, I judged, rightly or wrongly.
This is-- I knew I wouldn't get free.
Because somehow or other, the French knew I had a, had a false identification paper.
And they told the German that.
And so it was-- What was going to be the  lesser of two evils.
That's right, lesser of the two evils.
At that time, naturally, we didn't know what happens to Jews.
You know, you know, maybe, maybe we heard that they are going to work in Germany or something.
But we didn't know of the atrocities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they told us that from time to time, there is going to be on the board, names on it, for transport to work in Germany.
And they all said, you will be working in Germany.
You will be together with your families.
Well, I didn't have family then.
But some-- very often, when they got whole families together and [INAUDIBLE] children and this here, the children were together in one room.
The parents, the women were together in one room, and the men separately.
Not since then.
Not-- no, once I was arrested, no way.
But also when I was hiding up in the hills, I wouldn't go and tell them where I am, naturally.
And I think, according to the letters that I have, the last letter from my parents that I have was 1941, when they wrote to my brother, Eric.
It was in 1941, the last letter.
And they were saying, well, we didn't hear from Freddy for quite a while.
So therefore, since 1941, I wasn't able to correspond with them, or, or [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] The-- [SIGHS] they, they, they--
Put in different rooms.
And we always talked about escaping from there at night or something.
But I must say, we were really very heavily guarded there.
And we just could not escape.
We had-- on-- in October, 1943, the names came up again on our list.
And I was one of them to go on that transport.
I must say that in Drancy, I became very friendly to one doctor.
His name was Robert [?
Weis, ?] [?
Weis, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who I didn't know.
He was a professor.
And he was a very well-known medical professor.
And he worked there in, in the infirmary, et cetera.
But we, we really became friendly.
Somehow or other, I took to him.
To me, he was like my father.
He reminded me of my father, [?
Shalom. ?] And he-- we really became close friends.
And we got together on the same transport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were supposed to go and assemble on the 6th of October, 1943, in that-- on the big square of that, of that apartment house.
And they put us in rows of five and went into bus, a bus, buses.
There were 1,000 people on that transport, transport number 60.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were put into-- taken to the railway station in the buses, on the railway station, where loads and loads of SS soldiers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some with dogs and with whips in their hands.
And each bus in front of the, in front of the wagon, we saw these cattle wagons.
Funnily enough, on the cattle wagons were written, in French, [SPEAKING FRENCH]. 40 men or eight horses.
In each wagon was 100, 100 people, squeezed in.
And when there would seem to be no more room, the SS with whips squeezed us in in order to make space for the remainder.
There were men, women, and children, and old people, young people, all together.
We were able to take one hand luggage with us.
And we were just-- impossible for everybody to sit on the floor.
It was absolutely impossible.
There were two water buckets and one sanitary bucket in that-- in each wagon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] We organized ourselves.
We let the older people and the mothers with their children sit on the floor.
We youngsters, we stood.
And we tried to organize ourselves with regards to the sanitary bucket, you know?
And, and, and we each-- before we left, actually, we each had a piece of bread.
And we had some-- a piece of salami.
And before we left, we also had some soup.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they closed the wagons.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We went on this train for three days and three nights.
I don't know whether you can imagine the stench from the overflowed sanitary bucket, the moaning, the crying of the children, the fights between each other for more room.
It was really one of the worst thing that I experienced.
And we were completely degradated, degradated by the whole atmosphere.
To go and relieve yourself in the sanitary bucket in front of everybody, It is something terribly degrading.
And [SIGHS] it was an ordeal, an ordeal that we experienced, which I will never, ever forget.
When I saw and I saw the train, sequence of the train with Jews, I must tell you, this is something that affected me most, more than anything else.
And today-- no, I must say, I used to, used to dream a lot about this particular sequence in the train.
And it was something that I don't know whether anybody can understand it who has not experienced it.
I don't-- I can't imagine it.
Because the whole atmosphere was-- you become a sub-human to a certain extent.
The thoughts, why do they hit us?
We are human beings.
Why do they hit us?
Why are we-- why are we being treated just because we are Jews?
It is just unbelievable.
And we had these discussions during the train.
You know, you know, the train was nearly completely dark.
But up on top, there was a little window, which was barbed wired.

And the, the relationship between the inmates of this train was not terribly a friendly one, because everybody was just caring for themselves to survive, to, to be comfortable, as comfortable as one possibly can.
And if you-- God forbid, you stepped on somebody's toe or somebody's foot or something, it, it was abuse coming from him.
The anger, and, and-- it was something really unbelievable.
And I feel that this is the worst thing that-- one of the worst thing that remains in my memory forever.
